items.text: What is a business really worth? Add up all the cash it will ever hand back to its owners over its life, counting money years away as a bit less valuable than money today. That total is its 'intrinsic value' — and it has nothing to do with today's share price. Price is just the last number someone was willing to pay. Value is what the business actually produces.

'Profit' on paper and cash in the bank aren't the same. A company can report ₹100 of profit but only truly pocket ₹30 — the rest gets stuck in unsold stock, bills customers haven't paid yet, or new machines it had to buy. So when you see 'earnings', ask the better question: how much of it turned into real, spendable cash?

You can never pin a business's worth to the exact rupee — so don't try. Good investors say 'worth roughly ₹700–950', not 'exactly ₹847', and only buy when the price sits clearly below that range. Being roughly right and buying cheap beats being precisely wrong. As Buffett puts it: you don't need a man's exact weight to know he's overweight.
A company reports ₹80cr net profit. A friend says it should be worth 10× that = ₹800cr. What's the core flaw?
options: 10× is too low — quality firms deserve 20×
Profit ≠ cash; one year says nothing about durability or growth
He should use revenue, not profit
items.explanation: A multiple on one year of accounting profit ignores cash conversion, growth trajectory, and durability — the three things intrinsic value is actually made of.

items.text: Pidilite converts ~₹8 of every ₹10 of net profit into free cash flow. A typical steel maker converts ~₹4. What mostly explains the gap?
options: Steel needs heavy ongoing capex and working capital; adhesives don't
items.explanation: Capital intensity is the difference: heavy industry must reinvest most profits in plant and inventory just to keep running. Capital-light brands keep the cash.

items.text: A stock's price falls 30% in a month with no news about the business. What happened to its intrinsic value?
options: Almost nothing — price moved, value didn't
Impossible to say without the chart
items.explanation: Intrinsic value is the PV of future cash flows. If nothing changed about the business's cash generation, value is untouched — only the market's opinion moved.

items.text: Open Nestlé India on screener.in. In the annual numbers, find 'Net Profit' and 'Cash from Operating Activities' for the latest year. Are they roughly similar, or far apart? A company whose profit reliably shows up as real operating cash is higher quality than one where the two drift apart. Jot down which side Nestlé falls on — that's the cash-vs-profit check in the real world. (~5 min)
